words: ROB MOORE: I was thinking that you must have seen or heard of quite a few
Presidential assassinations over the years, haven1t you?
WILLIAM KAUDER: Oh yes.

speaker: ROB
words: Which ones do you remember?

speaker: WK
words: Well I don't know. I donft believe I understand you.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, do you remember how McKinley got assassinated?

speaker: WK
words: Oh yes it was awful. I remember from the...when was that first President

speaker: WK
words: assassinated? Lincoln was the first one that was shot.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, you werent born yet when Lincoln was shot were you?

speaker: WK
words: No, I wasn't born then. But I was the next one...Garfield.

speaker: ROB
words: Oh right, Garfield. Do you remember that one?

speaker: WK
words: Yeah. Dames A. Garfield. The song went, how was that now. (Sings)

speaker: WK
words: In My Saddest Day

speaker: WK
words: My name it is Charles Guiteau

speaker: WK
words: My name I'll never deny

speaker: WK
words: For the murder of James A. Garfield

speaker: WK
words: I am condemned to die.

speaker: ROB
words: Hm. Was that song popular shortly after Garfields murder?

speaker: WK
words: Um-hm.

speaker: ROB
words: Where were you when Garfield was murdered?

speaker: WK
words: We were living then in Missouri. In the northeast corner right almost at

speaker: WK
words: the mouth of the Des Moines.

speaker: ROB
words: Before Missouri, you lived in Illinois didn't you?

speaker: WK
words: I was born in Illinois, yes.

speaker: ROB
words: Where in Illinois?

speaker: WK
words: Adams county, not very far from Quincy.

speaker: ROB
words: Did your father know Abe Lincoln when he was an Illinois politican instead

speaker: ROB
words: of a President?

speaker: WK
words: Um-hm.

speaker: ROB
words: How did he know him?

speaker: WK
words: Well, principally by his speeches, you know. I think he seen him. When

speaker: WK
words: Lincoln first called for the first 75,000 men a brother of fathers, younger

speaker: WK
words: a couple years than father, he went on that call for the first 75,000. Father

speaker: WK
words: didn't go. But he went, a brother.

speaker: ROB
words: Did your father end up fighting in the Civil War?

speaker: WK
words: Um-hm. But I forget who he was with now. He had a...oh I don't know what

speaker: WK
words: you would call it but it was about that long (indicating one foot)...a paper

speaker: WK
words: that the names, you know, where they went across,, the country they went through,

speaker: WK
words: the companies they been taking. Rebels, all them, that was all names on that.

speaker: WK
words: It was lost. I don't know what become of it.

speaker: ROB
words: Was your father married before he went off to the Civil War?

speaker: WK
words: No.

speaker: ROB
words: After he came back he married your mother and they decided to move out

speaker: ROB
words: West?

speaker: WK
words: He married I think it was in 1867, I think. I was born in the last month

speaker: WK
words: of the year, 1868.

speaker: ROB
words: Well how old were you when your family made the move West?

speaker: WK
words: How old I was?

speaker: ROB
words: Yes.

speaker: WK
words: That was in 18...we come West in 1890. And I was just 21.

speaker: ROB
words: Why did you decide to move West?

speaker: WK
words: Father had an awful poor heart and the doctor told him that if he'd go West,

speaker: WK
words: if he'd live among the pines, that he would live ten years longer. And we

speaker: WK
words: come in 1890 and he died in 1900. Yes, he had an awful weak heart.

speaker: ROB
words: Were there a lot of people coming West at that time?

speaker: WK
words: Oh my gosh I think there was eighteen cars and they were all full.

speaker: WK
words: Immigrant train. We come on an immigrant ticket, it was a reduced rate. It

speaker: WK
words: begin on the 17th of March, St. Patrick's Day. We bought our ticket the 17th

speaker: WK
words: day of March and we come West.

speaker: ROB
words: How long did it take on the train?

speaker: WK
words: Well, we left on Monday, that was St. Patrick's Day was on Monday that

speaker: WK
words: year and we left, and I think it was Thursday, Wednesday or Thursday we got

speaker: WK
words: into Walla Walla. We bought our tickets for Walla Walla. We had a layover

speaker: WK
words: there at Pocatello. There was a train wreck or anyways the track was all tore

speaker: WK
words: up and we had to wait 'til they fixed that. It took about all day.

speaker: ROB
words: How did you get from Walla Walla to where you ended up on Cedar Creek?

speaker: WK
words: We landed in Walla Walla about the 23rd or 4th of March and I worked around

speaker: WK
words: there. The crops was all in, you couldn't work on a farm. But I did work

speaker: WK
words: for a farmer too, that is summer fallow. Then we moved all up to Dayton and

speaker: WK
words: father and I both worked in the sawmill there at Dayton up the Touchet about

speaker: WK
words: twelve, fourteen miles.

speaker: ROB
words: Up the what?

speaker: WK
words: Up the Touchet River. It runs through Dayton and down...I don't know where

speaker: WK
words: it comes out at Walla Walla. I don't know whether it empties into the Snake

speaker: WK
words: or the Columbia.

speaker: ROB
words: How long did you work there in that sawmill?

speaker: WK
words: We started in there...what time? Must have been in Dune when we went to

speaker: WK
words: work there in the sawmill. We worked there all fall and that winter we cut

speaker: WK
words: saw logs and made cordwood and so on. And in the spring then about Dune why...

speaker: WK
words: then I think in the spring we worked for that flume company.

speaker: ROB
words: The flume company?

speaker: WK
words: Uh-hm. Flume. They built a flume that run from the sawmill down to

speaker: WK
words: Dayton. It's about fifteen miles. And we had to build that flume and they

speaker: WK
words: flumed lumber down and cordwood. Made quite a lot of cordwood up there.

speaker: ROB
words: How much did you get paid for that sort of work?

speaker: WK
words: We got 30 dollars a month in the mill for working, and when the mill wasn't

speaker: WK
words: working and we was making cordwood why we got a dollar a cord. Boarded our

speaker: WK
words: selves. I forget...it was 30 dollars and board when we worked in the mill,

speaker: WK
words: or they allowed so much when we boarded ourselves. I forget how it was.

speaker: ROB
words: There was no union activity in the mills or anything in those days was there?

speaker: WK
words: I didn't hear of any, no. It came up to about $1.15 a day, to 30 dollars

speaker: WK
words: a month and board. We had to work eleven hours.

speaker: ROB
words: Were you pretty satisfied with that?

speaker: WK
words: Well we had to be. We either had to work or starve. No we wasn't satisfied

speaker: WK
words: with that but we had to be.

speaker: ROB
words: When did you move up into the Cedar Creek country?

speaker: ROB
words: And you took up a homestead then, didn't you?

speaker: WK
words: Uh-hm. Father took up a homestead right in the neighborhood. It wasn't

speaker: WK
words: much. It was a nice piece of land but it wasn't much, it was only about 40

speaker: WK
words: acres. And then I took up a 160 not far from that. But my gosh it was

speaker: WK
words: heavily timbered.

speaker: ROB
words: What was the land like around there then?

speaker: WK
words: The land? If you got any in cultivation it produced good. But it was a

speaker: WK
words: job getting in any cultivation. There were big pines on there. Gosh we cut

speaker: WK
words: down them pine trees, oh be great tall trees. Saw 'em up into about 10 or

speaker: WK
words: 12 foot logs. Roll 'em together and make a big pile and burn it.

speaker: ROB
words: Was there a lot of underbrush in the forest in those days?

speaker: WK
words: In some places was an awful lot. It was pretty hard to work it too, it's

speaker: WK
words: called that sarvis you know, sarvis berry.

speaker: ROB
words: About how long would it take you to break in some land? How long did you

speaker: ROB
words: have to work on your homestead place until it was pretty comfortable?

speaker: WK
words: Well we mostly had I guess four or five years before we got it so that

speaker: WK
words: we could help ourselves pretty good. 'Course we got small patches in and had

speaker: WK
words: pretty good garden. Raised all our vegatables.

speaker: ROB
words: Was it hard work proving up on a homestead?

speaker: WK
words: I'll say it was. I'd never do it over again.

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of things did you have to do to prove up?

speaker: WK
words: Well we had to live five years on the place. And we had to have so much

speaker: WK
words: in cultivation. I forget now how that was. I think it required continusous

speaker: WK
words: residence, I ain't sure but I think it did.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you build yourself a little cabin there on the land or what?

speaker: WK
words: To live on?

speaker: ROB
words: Yes.

speaker: WK
words: Yes, we built us a log house with three rooms in it. A living room and

speaker: WK
words: two bedrooms. We added on two more rooms, a kitchen and another bedroom.

speaker: ROB
words: When you moved up to Cedar Creek how did you know which land was open

speaker: ROB
words: and which was already taken?

speaker: WK
words: When we lived there in the canyon working at that sawmill there was

speaker: WK
words: another family that lived there by the name of Wells. And Mr. Wells and I

speaker: WK
words: went up in the fall before. We'd heard so much about his Cavendish and

speaker: WK
words: Cedar Creek country that we thought we'd come and look it over and we did,

speaker: WK
words: and we kind of picked out our homestead.

speaker: ROB
words: Did the Wells end up moving down with you then too?

speaker: WK
words: Yes, Wells moved in by gosh in an awful brushy patch. He took up a 160.

speaker: ROB
words: How come your father only took up 40?

speaker: WK
words: Well it happened to be right in the neighborhood you know. And wasn't

speaker: WK
words: much brush on it. It laid nice and was in a nice shape. A nice piece of

speaker: WK
words: land but it was kind of bottled up, that is other places was taken all

speaker: WK
words: around it. But we happened to buy a way out. Father being of such a weak

speaker: WK
words: heart why we didn't want to stick ourselves in too much of a brush patch.

speaker: ROB
words: Isn't it kind of a strange thing for a man with a weak heart to go out

speaker: ROB
words: and start a homestead?

speaker: WK
words: It took a lot of courage I'll tell ya.

speaker: ROB
words: Why do you think most people were homesteading at that time?

speaker: WK
words: Well all them prairie farms, they were all homestead. Dust like that

speaker: WK
words: reservation when it opened. Well it's all homestead. Now that Nez Perce

speaker: WK
words: reservation and the Camas Prairie reservation all opened for homestead. Why

speaker: WK
words: they wanted them all settled up by whites is a mystery to me yet. They'd

speaker: WK
words: better left that with the Indians.

speaker: ROB
words: Well what would cause most people to want to be homesteaders? Why would

speaker: ROB
words: a person want to leave the things they knew and go out and be a homesteader

speaker: ROB
words: in a strange land?

speaker: WK
words: Well some for different reasons. Some on account of change of climate,

speaker: WK
words: perhaps it's better for their health. Now like father with that weak heart

speaker: WK
words: of course the change was better for him. The home we had back East was

speaker: WK
words: worth a whole township of this country.

speaker: ROB
words: The home you had back East was worth a whole township of this country?

speaker: WK
words: It was for living and things. Yes they could make a living on it so much

speaker: WK
words: easier.

speaker: ROB
words: Well why did you choose to leave then?

speaker: WK
words: There was only the three of us in the family. There was father and myself

speaker: WK
words: and mother. I couldn't go away and leave father in the condition he was in.

speaker: WK
words: I stayed with both of them until they passed away. Never married 'til after

speaker: WK
words: wards.

speaker: ROB
words: So you kind of helped your father prove up on his place too?

speaker: WK
words: Oh yes.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you build a house on your place also?

speaker: WK
words: Yes I built a log house on that. Wasn't no lumber around. I had to build

speaker: WK
words: a log house. And a log barn. I cleared up some land on it. And then I

speaker: WK
words: didn't have it more than a year or two 'til father passed away. And that

speaker: WK
words: left mother alone and I had to stay there with mother most of the time.

speaker: WK
words: And so I sold off the homestead.

speaker: ROB
words: How much did you get for it?

speaker: WK
words: Not very much. Dust about enough to prove up on it if I wanted to prove

speaker: WK
words: up.

speaker: ROB
words: Did quite a few other people move into the Cedar Creek area at the same

speaker: ROB
words: time you did?

speaker: WK
words: Most of that country was taken up. There was quite a big settlement in

speaker: WK
words: there at the time we went in. And they kept proving up and mortgaging and

speaker: WK
words: this D. P. Vollmer, you've heard of him?

speaker: ROB
words: Yes.

speaker: WK
words: He'd take mortgages on all those places for backing for his bank.

speaker: WK
words: They'd go down to Lewiston prove up and mortgage the place and away they'd

speaker: WK
words: go.

speaker: ROB
words: They'd just leave?

speaker: WK
words: The country was well settled when we first went in there and five years

speaker: WK
words: later there was just a few of us that was in there.

speaker: ROB
words: What happened to everybody else?

speaker: WK
words: Well they proved up mortgaged and left. Didn't want to clear all that

speaker: WK
words: land.

speaker: ROB
words: Well why would they homestead in the first place if they didn't want to

speaker: ROB
words: clear all that land?

speaker: WK
words: For a fortune. 'Cause they thought they could sell out to a big price.

speaker: WK
words: And it didn't prove too good.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, your neighbors who stayed what were they like?

speaker: WK
words: They were good people good neighbors. They all come from different countries.

speaker: WK
words: Some from Texas, Arkansas, Illinois.

speaker: ROB
words: Did they all have different reasons for coming?

speaker: WK
words: I suppose. I don't know. There was people you know from different nations

speaker: WK
words: that gathered there and they never asked ore another any questions. They

speaker: WK
words: was just neighbors and that was all. It was none of my business what my

speaker: WK
words: neighbor was into before or what he done or how he lived or anything. They

speaker: WK
words: just seemed be all real nice.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you become friends with them later?

speaker: WK
words: Oh yes, yeah.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you remember the names of your neighbors? Of specific neighbors of

speaker: ROB
words: yours?

speaker: WK
words: Well yes some of *em. Are you acquainted any in Kendrick?

speaker: ROB
words: Some yes.

speaker: WK
words: You know the Longs? Well, they had a homestead up there.

speaker: ROB
words: Mr. Long was a meat cutter wasn't he?

speaker: WK
words: Afterwards, yes. The oldest boy, Dodie.

speaker: ROB
words: Well when they were on Cedar Creek was he a homesteader?

speaker: WK
words: Yes, he had a homestead there on Cedar Creek and some of them boys were

speaker: WK
words: born there. There were four boys and I don't know, one or two of 'em were

speaker: WK
words: born there. I believe this Marvin that's living there in Kendrick now was

speaker: WK
words: born there. And maybe one just before him.

speaker: ROB
words: Did Long have a pretty nice homestead?

speaker: WK
words: Yes pretty good homestead. He built a pretty good house on it and I guess

speaker: WK
words: it brought a pretty good price too at that time. Four or five thousand

speaker: WK
words: dollars. But the party that bought it last, I don't know how they got mixed

speaker: WK
words: up but the kids they went to Kendrick to school. Lived there at home and went

speaker: WK
words: down in the truck. Some way or another the house caught fire and burnt up.

speaker: WK
words: And it's a wreck now. It's funny, there was three pretty good buildings that

speaker: WK
words: burnt down up there in that country. On the Wells place there was a cellar,

speaker: WK
words: wood shed that was pretty good and the house was in pretty good shape and it

speaker: WK
words: burnt. And then on the Cramer place was an awful good frame house there with

speaker: WK
words: a basement there and it burnt down. And now this here old place of Long's it

speaker: WK
words: burnt. They were all about...it wasn't for the timber you could see the

speaker: WK
words: places. The Long's place, and the Cramer place you can see one house and

speaker: WK
words: the other. But they hadn't cleared up that much land. But the Wells place

speaker: WK
words: back further, you couldn't see it.

speaker: ROB
words: Were most of the homesteaders single men or married men?

speaker: WK
words: No, married men.

speaker: ROB
words: Did most of them bring their families with...

speaker: WK
words: They'd take what was left.

speaker: ROB
words: Were they of different nationalities too, from different countries?

speaker: WK
words: No, I don't know. Oh yes I guess there were. The Germans, Irish, English.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you think most of the homesteaders were pretty religious people?

speaker: WK
words: Yes, Longs was quite religious, Wells was quite religious. But there was

speaker: WK
words: a few in there like Thompson, and Halls, and some of those and a few others

speaker: WK
words: they wasn't very religious. We'd have church once in awhile in the old

speaker: WK
words: schoolhouse.

speaker: ROB
words: Would most people attend church whether they were religious or not or

speaker: ROB
words: whether they belonged to a different kind of church or not?

speaker: WK
words: Well they might have belonged to different churches but they all attended

speaker: WK
words: whenever they could get a preacher in the schoolhouse there. The neighbors

speaker: WK
words: would all turn out and help. They had Sunday School, There's nothing like

speaker: WK
words: that going on there now.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you remember when Governor Steunenberg was killed?

speaker: WK
words: Yeah.

speaker: ROB
words: What was people's reaction around here like?

speaker: WK
words: Well you couldn't get the sentiment of most of the people, they kept

speaker: WK
words: pretty quiet about it. Steunenberg was...some of them didn't believe in

speaker: WK
words: the way Steunenberg treated those miners. Had em in the bull pen there.

speaker: WK
words: 'Course they blowed up that mine. Wrecked the whole thing.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you think most people sympathized with the miners or with the mine

speaker: ROB
words: owners during that trouble?

speaker: WK
words: Well they didn't express their thoughts very much.

speaker: ROB
words: What did you think about it?

speaker: WK
words: Well the way I think is the way Ithink about all things. Nobody has the

speaker: WK
words: right to blow up somebody elses property. I don't believe in that. I

speaker: WK
words: have no right to go over to your place and blow your house to pieces or

speaker: WK
words: your barns or anything. No more than Iwant somebody to blow up the buildings

speaker: WK
words: I have. I don't believe in that. Do you? What do you think about it?

speaker: ROB
words: I don't believe in that either, but my sympathies are kind of with the

speaker: ROB
words: miners, with the workers, rather than with the owners of the mines. But

speaker: ROB
words: not counting the blowing up of the mine...

speaker: WK
words: You don't believe in that? (No.) I didn't either.

speaker: ROB
words: How did you feel about the rest of the things that they were doing, striking

speaker: ROB
words: and trying to get better pay? How did you feel about the trail of the men

speaker: ROB
words: who were accused of killing Steunenberg? Do you remember that trail?

speaker: ROB
words: I don't...let's see, no I didn't think much of that trail. They had a

speaker: ROB
words: couple other men up for killing Steunenberg besides Orchard. And it seems

speaker: ROB
words: like some way and another got it stuck on Orchard altogether. Iforget what

speaker: ROB
words: then...there was two others, I believe.

speaker: ROB
words: One of them was Big Bill Haywood.

speaker: WK
words: Yes, oh yes that's right. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: ROB
words: How do you think most people, most of the earlier setters felt about the

speaker: ROB
words: law?

speaker: WK
words: I never heard of them express their...

speaker: ROB
words: How did you feel about it?

speaker: WK
words: Well of course if government give 'em the land away why they ought to comply

speaker: WK
words: with the law.

speaker: ROB
words: It wasn't easy to have the laws enforced in that homestead country though

speaker: ROB
words: was it?

speaker: WK
words: No they had no trouble. I don't think that they had any contesting done.

speaker: WK
words: I think everybody complied pretty well with the law.

speaker: ROB
words: Well like during Prohibition weren't there quite a few moonshiners up

speaker: ROB
words: there on Cedar Creek?

speaker: WK
words: Oh that Prohibition that was a fake.

speaker: ROB
words: That was a fake?

speaker: WK
words: Yeah. (Laughs.) Made lots of moonshine. I worked for a fella that

speaker: WK
words: was making moonshine. I told him I says to hide your thing and keep it

speaker: WK
words: away until we get through here. And then do your moonshining. I didn't like

speaker: WK
words: it.

speaker: ROB
words: You didn't like the moonshine?

speaker: WK
words: No.

speaker: ROB
words: Why not?

speaker: WK
words: Well I don't think it's right. It's against the law and people should

speaker: WK
words: abide by the law.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you think that mooshine law was about the worst law that they've had

speaker: ROB
words: around here or has there been worse ones than that? Or ones that were broken

speaker: ROB
words: more than that one was?

speaker: WK
words: Well I don't know about any other times. But of course that moonshine was

speaker: WK
words: breaking the law all right.

speaker: ROB
words: What would you do for fun when you had a little spare time and you thought

speaker: ROB
words: you might want to go do something? What would you do in the early days around

speaker: WK
words: Well all the spare time that we could gather up why we worked burning out

speaker: WK
words: stumps or clearing up the ground. I'll tell you it was quite a job. Now we

speaker: WK
words: had one patch there, five acres. I had it in timothy and it was awful

speaker: WK
words: heavy. Timothy was way tall and heavy on the ground and we had to scythe

speaker: WK
words: it on account of the stumps, we couldn't get no machine in there. You know

speaker: WK
words: that was blamed hard work. Then rake it up by hand. Even a small patch like

speaker: WK
words: five acres, some of that was heavy enough looked like it'd go about three

speaker: WK
words: tons to the acre. Gosh you'd have pile shock after shock. We had awful good

speaker: WK
words: crops them times.

speaker: ROB
words: What kinds of crops did you grow?

speaker: WK
words: Well we grewed wheat and oats.

speaker: ROB
words: What would you do for fun? Would you go out and burn stumps for fun or

speaker: ROB
words: was there something else you'd do when you wanted to have a good time?

speaker: WK
words: Burn stumps? We couldn't burn any, only the pines they pitchy enough to

speaker: WK
words: burn. And them others we had...I got one of the neighbors to come over. I

speaker: WK
words: had five acres there that I'd cut off and I had it broke. The stumps were

speaker: WK
words: so darn thick on it I couldn't do anything and the neighbor he was quite a

speaker: WK
words: man with this stump powder. So I got a box of stump powder and got him to

speaker: WK
words: blow 'em out. He blowed out a bunch of stumps for me, looked like I was

speaker: WK
words: clearing land again. My I had a mess on the ground.

speaker: ROB
words: About how many years of work would it take to bring a homestead up to

speaker: ROB
words: where it was pretty comfortable?

speaker: WK
words: Oh that's different in different places. Some places where there was no

speaker: WK
words: red firs or white firs or anything like that to fool with, just the pines,

speaker: WK
words: you could burn them pines out and get quite a field in a short time. Now

speaker: WK
words: take across the canyon from us over in what's called the Gold Hill country.

speaker: WK
words: On that ground it's principally more pines grew. They just burn the pine

speaker: WK
words: stumps out and they have lots of clear land in a short time. But we couldn't

speaker: WK
words: do that. We had too many firs. And them firs they rooted out for looked to

speaker: WK
words: be over an acre of ground.

speaker: ROB
words: How long did it take you to get your place pretty well fixed up?

speaker: WK
words: Oh took quite a long time. I had to work out so much to make a living.

speaker: ROB
words: What kinds of work would you do when you worked out?

speaker: WK
words: Well I worked out in harvest on someones thrashing machine, twenty or

speaker: WK
words: thirty days. And then sometimes in the spring of the year as soon as I

speaker: WK
words: got my crop in I took my team and I worked on the road. I got four dollars

speaker: WK
words: a day for that. I think I made 75 or 100 dollars after I got the crop in,

speaker: WK
words: working on the road. And then I went out in harvest.

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of work would you do on the road?

speaker: WK
words: Grading. I had my team on the road and was grading, principal part of

speaker: WK
words: the time.

speaker: ROB
words: Would you follow the harvest around different parts of the county?

speaker: WK
words: No. I worked with a thrashing machine and we'd get into our community

speaker: WK
words: and thrash that out and get into another community. That way I kind of got

speaker: WK
words: acquainted with people and the country.

speaker: ROB
words: What town would you usually go to for supplies?

speaker: WK
words: Oh at first we had to go to Kendrick principally. And then Leland sprung

speaker: WK
words: up then we had a good store there at Leland. Winters and Godsworth, they had

speaker: WK
words: a big store.

speaker: ROB
words: Winters and Godsworth?

speaker: WK
words: Uh-hm.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you like Leland more than Kendrick?

speaker: WK
words: Well it was just handier. We didn't have no grade to pull when we come

speaker: WK
words: home. Go to Leland, we could buy just about as cheap at Leland as we could

speaker: WK
words: at Kendrick and we didn't have that grade to pull coming home. That grade

speaker: WK
words: from Cedar Creek down to Kendrick was a holy fright.

speaker: ROB
words: Did many people have trouble on that grade?

speaker: WK
words: Oh yes. Sometimes it was so bad it took four horses to pull an empty

speaker: WK
words: wagon back up.

speaker: ROB
words: How does it feel to be 105 years old?

speaker: WK
words: Feels like you're worth nothing.

speaker: ROB
words: Feels like what?

speaker: WK
words: Feels like you're worthless. Better be dead.

speaker: ROB
words: No. You've lived through such a remarkable period of time.

speaker: WK
words: Yes. I even got so important that I got a letter from the President, Nixon.

speaker: WK
words: A personal letter.

speaker: ROB
words: What did he have to say?

speaker: WK
words: Oh he congratulated me, you know, and talked about if we had lots such

speaker: WK
words: citizens we'd have a good country. Oh he made a certainly nice letter. One

speaker: WK
words: of my friends had to read the letter to me. She keeps the letter. If I

speaker: WK
words: had the letter here I'd let you read it. But I wouldn't know...they laid it

speaker: WK
words: away for me I wouldn't know where it was. I couldn't see it. Tell you, when

speaker: WK
words: a person as blind as this, same as nothing. It's the most disagreeable

speaker: WK
words: feeling that I've ever witnessed.

speaker: ROB
words: What do you think stands out most in your life? What do you think is been

speaker: ROB
words: the most important thing in your life over the past 100 years?

speaker: WK
words: I don't know. I didn't think one thing was more important than another.

speaker: WK
words: It was all about the same.

speaker: ROB
words: When the changes were happening, you know, like the changes from the horse

speaker: ROB
words: steam, and steam to car, and all those sorts of tnings, did they seem like

speaker: ROB
words: they happened real fast or did it seem like it happened pretty slow?

speaker: WK
words: Well sir, I don't know hardly. In some ways this change maybe it's all

speaker: WK
words: right. But the way that it's a'going now I'm afraid it's going to have trouble,

speaker: WK
words: Everything is getting so unmerciful high, unreasonable high. When I first

speaker: WK
words: landed on Cedar Creek, when Cleveland was President, you could buy a good

speaker: WK
words: cow for $12. Here lately I've heard some of them say that they got $150

speaker: WK
words: for a common cow. Beef steak is out of sight. Pork, you never see any.

speaker: WK
words: I haven't had a bite a pork for since I left Alma Setts', that I recognized

speaker: WK
words: as pork.

speaker: ROB
words: What do you remember as being the happiest period of your life?

speaker: WK
words: It was about when I was sixteen, seventeen I guess.

speaker: ROB
words: Why would you say that time?

speaker: WK
words: Well after that you got eighteen, twenty I had to dig in. I worked like

speaker: WK
words: the dickens.

speaker: ROB
words: Were you glad that you moved west?

speaker: WK
words: No, I'd rather remained where we was. I always kind of liked to farm

speaker: WK
words: and out West we had no chance for farming and back East we did. If I didn't

speaker: WK
words: have enough ground I could rent a few acres from the neighbor. Lots of time

speaker: WK
words: I rented 30 or 40 acre from the neighbor for corn. I would cultivate and

speaker: WK
words: work that and have the home place in grain.

speaker: ROB
words: That was back in Missouri?

speaker: WK
words: Um-hm.
